---
title: "1.2 Common Pitfalls of Backtesting"
subtitle: "Chapter 01. Backtesting and Automated Execution"

format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: show
    code-tools: false
include-after-body:
  - ../../../../styles/web-note-modal.html
---

::: {.section-output-links}
[Slides](video-lesson-slides.qmd){.action-video aria-label="Open video lesson slides" data-modal="video" data-label="Video lesson slides" data-tooltip="Slides"}
<span aria-hidden="true">/</span>
[PDF](pdf-notes.qmd){.action-pdf aria-label="Open PDF notes" data-tooltip="PDF"}
<span aria-hidden="true">/</span>
[Markdown](downloads/section.json){.action-markdown aria-label="Open raw markdown viewer" data-modal="markdown" data-label="Markdown" data-tooltip="Markdown" data-markdown-src="downloads/section.json"}
<span aria-hidden="true">/</span>
[Bundle](downloads/section-bundle.zip){.action-bundle aria-label="Download markdown bundle" data-tooltip="Bundle"}
:::

# Backtesting Pitfalls Make History Too Easy

Most backtesting pitfalls point in the same direction: they make historical
performance look better than the strategy could have achieved in live trading.
That is why they are dangerous. They do not merely add random measurement
error; they can convert a market-access problem, data problem, or modeling
problem into apparent alpha.

The pitfalls in this section share one diagnostic question. Did the backtest
give the strategy information, securities, prices, or venues that the live
strategy would not have had at the same decision time? If yes, the backtest is
describing a friendlier market than the one the trader would actually face.

# Keep Future Information Out of Current Signals

Look-ahead bias occurs when a current trading signal uses information from the
future. A same-day high or low is the simplest example. Before the trading day
ends, the final high and low are not known. If an intraday entry rule uses
those values during the same day, the backtest is no longer testing a live
decision rule.

This bias is essentially a programming error. It can infect a backtest because
historical data are already complete, but it cannot infect a live program in the
same form because live code cannot request tomorrow's data. A strong defense is
therefore architectural: make the backtest and live system share the same
strategy logic, and change only the data feed. The live feed disciplines the
rule because it exposes only information that exists at the decision time.

# Prevent Validation Data From Becoming Training Data

Data-snooping bias appears when a model has enough freedom to fit accidental
historical patterns. A formal out-of-sample test is supposed to protect against
this. The protection fails when the researcher sees a poor validation result,
changes parameters or rules, and tests the same validation period again. After
that loop, the validation data have become part of training.

Cross-validation can help when the data set is small or when one fixed holdout
period would be arbitrary. The model is trained and checked across multiple
subsets, and the researcher asks whether performance is stable rather than
dependent on one favorable slice of history. A high Sharpe ratio and short
maximum drawdown duration make this stability easier to observe because fewer
subsamples are dominated by long losing periods.

The final defense is walk-forward testing. Chan emphasizes that some
data-snooping can creep in even when the research process is careful. A frozen
model therefore needs evidence from data that arrive after research stops.
Paper trading is useful, and a small real-money deployment can reveal execution
issues that paper trading still misses.

# Prefer Simplicity Until Complexity Earns Its Keep

Model simplicity is a defense against data-snooping because every additional
rule, parameter, threshold, or distributional assumption is another way to fit
historical noise. A model with few numerical parameters can still overfit if it
contains many hand-built exceptions. The practical standard is therefore not
just "few parameters," but fewer opportunities to encode accidents in the
sample.

This is the reason for Chan's preference for linearity. A linear model is not
automatically true, and financial markets do not have to be linear. The point
is that a simple linear rule is easier to explain, easier to validate, and less
able to memorize noise. A nonlinear model should have a strong theoretical and
empirical reason for existing; otherwise, the simpler approximation deserves to
be tested first.

Linearity also applies to position sizing and factor combination. In a
mean-reverting model, a linear allocation scales the position with the deviation
from the mean instead of waiting for a hard threshold. In a factor model,
normalized predictors can be combined with deliberately simple weights. For a
factor $f_i$, the normalized value is:

$$
z_i = \frac{f_i - \operatorname{mean}(f_i)}
           {\operatorname{std}(f_i)}
$$

Equal-weight or signed-rank combinations will not always be best. Their value
is that they force every extra weighting choice to justify the added
data-snooping risk.

# Adjust Corporate Actions Before Signals Use Prices

Stock splits and dividends can create false signals when the historical price
series is not adjusted. In an $N$-for-1 split, the share price falls by a factor
of $N$, but the shareholder owns $N$ times as many shares. The market value is
unchanged. If the pre-split prices are not adjusted, the backtest can mistake
an accounting event for a large economic price move.

Dividends create the same kind of problem. On the ex-dividend date, the stock
price typically drops by roughly the dividend amount, while the shareholder
receives the distribution. A price-only signal that ignores the dividend
adjustment may interpret this mechanical drop as a trading opportunity.

These adjustments matter in live trading as well as in backtesting. If a live
strategy uses historical lookbacks, the historical prices must be adjusted
before the market opens on the relevant ex-date. Otherwise, the live signal can
inherit the same false jump that would have distorted the historical test.

Chan also makes the implementation timing explicit. The adjustment is not a
cleanup step that can wait until later in the day. It has to be applied before
the market opens on the ex-date so that a live model sees a continuous
historical series when it computes its morning signal. The same logic applies
to reverse splits, ETFs, and, with more complicated handling, options.

# Use a Universe That Includes Failures

Survivorship bias occurs when the historical database includes only securities
that still exist today. A backtest built on that data acts as if the trader
knew in advance which distressed companies would survive.

This is especially damaging for long-only mean-reversion strategies. A rule
that buys the stock with the largest recent drop may look profitable if the
database excludes companies that later went bankrupt or delisted. The surviving
stocks are precisely the ones that recovered enough to remain visible.

The direction of the bias depends on the strategy. Short-only mean-reversion
can look worse because bankrupt stocks, which would have been strong short
trades, are missing. Long-short mean-reversion may experience both effects, but
the bias is not eliminated. The preferred solution is a survivorship-bias-free
database. If that is unavailable, limiting the test to more recent history can
reduce the damage, but it does not solve the problem.

Momentum strategies are affected differently. When failed stocks disappear from
the database, some profitable short momentum trades disappear with them, so the
measured return can be biased downward instead of upward. That difference does
not make survivorship bias harmless. It means the sign and magnitude of the
distortion depend on the trading logic, so the researcher cannot assume the
bias is conservative.

# Model Short-Sale Constraints When the Strategy Shorts

A stock strategy that shorts securities assumes those securities can be
borrowed. In live trading, the broker has to locate shares and arrange a stock
loan. Some stocks are hard to borrow because the float is small, short interest
is already high, or lenders demand compensation. In extreme cases, the desired
quantity cannot be borrowed at all.

Ignoring this constraint can inflate a backtest. A model may appear to profit
from shorting names that other traders could not short in size, or could short
only with a high borrow cost. Rule-based restrictions can also matter. For
example, short-sale rules may prevent a short market order from being filled
under certain conditions.

The data problem is difficult because a historically accurate hard-to-borrow
list depends on the broker and time period. The practical response is to treat
small-cap short results with extra skepticism, include borrow costs and locate
availability when data are available, and remember that ETFs can also become
hard to borrow.

Chan highlights the uptick rules as another source of execution mismatch. Under
the original uptick rule, and later under the alternative uptick rule after a
circuit breaker is triggered, a short sale may have to occur above the last
trade or national best bid. That can make a short market order unexecutable at
the moment the backtest assumes the fill. A historically careful short backtest
therefore needs to model not only direction and borrow, but also the short-sale
rules that were in effect at the time.

# Match Historical Prices to the Order You Would Send

U.S. stocks trade on many venues, including primary exchanges, ECNs, and dark
pools. Standard daily open and close prices often come from consolidated data,
which record the first or last regular-hours execution across venues. That is
not necessarily the same price a market-on-open (MOO) or market-on-close (MOC)
order would receive.

MOO and MOC orders are routed to the primary exchange auction. If a strategy
uses those order types, the backtest should use primary-exchange auction prices.
Using consolidated prices can create unrealistic fills, especially for
mean-reversion strategies. A small trade on a secondary venue can print far
from the primary auction price, and the next price may revert from that
unrepresentative outlier.

The same concern applies to high and low prices. Historical highs and lows are
usually consolidated values and may be produced by small trades away from the
primary venue. If the strategy cannot execute at those prices in meaningful
size, they should not be treated as reliable fills.

# Treat Forex Quotes as Venue-Specific

Currency markets are even more fragmented than equity markets. There is no rule
requiring a trade on one venue to occur at the best bid or ask available across
all venues. A foreign-exchange backtest is realistic only when its historical
quotes come from the same venue, or executable venue set, that the live
strategy can access.

This matters for both signals and costs. Trade prices and sizes are often
unavailable or delayed, so bid-ask quotes are usually the safer basis for forex
backtesting. Those quotes still vary across venues. As a result, spread and
transaction-cost assumptions are venue-specific, not universal.

# Pitfall Map

| Pitfall                 | Backtest mistake                                  | Practical defense                                           |
| ----------------------- | ------------------------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------------- |
| Look-ahead bias         | Use information unavailable at decision time      | Share strategy logic and delay signals until observable     |
| Data-snooping bias      | Tune rules until validation data look good        | Use simple models, cross-validation, and walk-forward tests |
| Corporate actions       | Treat splits or dividends as economic price moves | Adjust historical prices before signals use them            |
| Survivorship bias       | Exclude delisted securities                       | Use survivorship-bias-free data                             |
| Short-sale constraints  | Assume every short can be borrowed and filled     | Model locate availability, borrow cost, and short-sale rules |
| Consolidated prices     | Test auction orders on non-auction prints         | Use primary-exchange prices for MOO and MOC strategies      |
| Forex venue mismatch    | Test with quotes from venues you cannot trade     | Match quotes and spreads to executable venues               |

# Review Checklist

- Does every signal use only information known at the decision time?
- Was the model changed after seeing validation results?
- Is the rule simple enough that each extra modeling choice has a clear reason?
- Are split and dividend adjustments applied before the strategy uses the
  historical lookback?
- Does the equity universe include delisted securities where the strategy would
  have traded them?
- Are short-sale availability, borrow costs, and relevant short-sale rules
  represented?
- Do open, close, high, and low prices match the execution venue and order type?
- Do currency quotes and spreads come from venues the live strategy can access?

# Reading Path

- Previous: [1.1 The Importance of Backtesting](../01-the-importance-of-backtesting/web-notes.qmd)
- Next: [1.3 Statistical Significance of Backtesting](../03-statistical-significance-of-backtesting/web-notes.qmd)
